title: podman pull for different platforms replaces images
body:
### Issue Description

i need a image for multiple platforms, so i started to pull it with different platformtags
while doing so i took note that `podman pull` will replace the existing tag with a new tag on each of those pulls


### Steps to reproduce the issue

```
$ podman pull  quay.io/fedora/fedora-silverblue:42 
$ podman pull --platform linxux/aarch64 quay.io/fedora/fedora-silverblue:42 
$ podman images
REPOSITORY                                           TAG         IMAGE ID      CREATED        SIZE
<none>                                               <none>      9bff1459cfe6  10 hours ago   5.41 GB
quay.io/fedora/fedora-silverblue                     42          54c407e3f2dc  10 hours ago   5.44 GB

$ podman pull quay.io/fedora/fedora-silverblue:42 
Trying to pull quay.io/fedora/fedora-silverblue:42...
Getting image source signatures
...
Copying config 9bff1459cf done   | 
Writing manifest to image destination
9bff1459cfe67c2556eb90ee9608034bd3b0ccc4345ec06451e1a2611ed1cef5

$ podman images
REPOSITORY                                           TAG         IMAGE ID      CREATED        SIZE
quay.io/fedora/fedora-silverblue                     42          9bff1459cfe6  10 hours ago   5.41 GB
<none>                                               <none>      54c407e3f2dc  10 hours ago   5.44 GB
```

### Describe the results you received

podman simply replaces the tag

### Describe the results you expected

podman keeps per platform tags on a image

### podman info output

comments.body:
I reproduced this and it certainly doesn't seem like the intended functionality

```bash
$ podman images
REPOSITORY                        TAG         IMAGE ID      CREATED       SIZE
quay.io/fedora/fedora-silverblue  42          7b232f6a3738  19 hours ago  5.44 GB
<none>                            <none>      d5e45a44caee  19 hours ago  5.41 GB
``` 

I also tried to pull both without giving either of them a tag and the same overwriting occurs but with the `latest` tag
```bash
REPOSITORY                        TAG         IMAGE ID      CREATED       SIZE
quay.io/fedora/fedora-silverblue  latest      7b232f6a3738  20 hours ago  5.44 GB
<none>                            <none>      d5e45a44caee  20 hours ago  5.41 GB
```

Is there a reason you don't give them different tags? @RonnyPfannschmidt 

```bash
$ podman images
REPOSITORY                        TAG         IMAGE ID      CREATED       SIZE
quay.io/fedora/fedora-silverblue  42          7b232f6a3738  19 hours ago  5.44 GB
quay.io/fedora/fedora-silverblue  43          0bb117f183aa  40 hours ago  5.48 GB
```

well - they are different target platforms/architectures of the same manifest - i don’t really want to invent a own local naming scheme - i just want to be able to pull them from the official name and have the tags be valid for the pulled platforms

> i don’t really want to invent a own local naming scheme

That makes sense

@Luap99 what do you think a good naming scheme for this should be?

* tag1: 42
* tag2: 42-linux/aarch64
/assign
why use different name? practically its the same manifest - id much rather see a new field for platform and have a image "twice"

my preferred mechanism would be that podman takes the manifest as well and allows different arch images to have the same tag

based on a preliminary skimming of the involved deps/vendored packages, that may be utterly painful to implement

There is a very long history about this behavior (it matches what docker does) and we cannot change it just like that. That would be a major breaking change for a lot of users. I don't have the time to dig out the other issues on this repo but I am sure this is already tracked somewhere else.

I do acknowledge that the design is not particular sane when someone has to deal with multi platform images in parallel but it is how it was started since multi platform images (manifest lists) only came later. And from memory the main reason why it is not easy to chnage is that many images actually (maybe used to) report incorrect arch values. So even if we would extend our logic to store several names and lookup based on the arch it will break for images that contain incorrect values.

So if something like this could even be changed would need a lot of analysis across the entire ecosystem and researching past issues, etc... to come up with a design that doesn't just break a lot of people. And even then it would likely require a major release (i.e. podman 6 next year)

https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/12682 contains some relevant discussion around this.

I think for this issue it is fair to evaluate if we should change our store to be platform (manifest) aware somehow.

Would it be feasible to be manifest list aware 

So that different images of the same manifest may share names

Im entirely okay if this needs opt in configuration and/or a major release

I'm a little out of my depth here but I did some research and have some thoughts.

I agree that optimally the image store should be manifest list aware. It sounds like these are the major hurdles to that:
* It's likely a breaking change
* Many images have incorrect platform data
* Images will no longer be unique with just repo:tag they will also need platform to be considered unique

I can think of two options to get the ball rolling
1. Add a warning when an image is being overwritten so that users are at least aware of what's happening in these situations
2. Update local image storage to contain platform data but don't use it for anything yet. This way if/when we want to tackle this issue completely we will have the data easily available. And if the platform data isn't used for anything yet it won't break anything.

Ultimately I think it would be possible to have the different platforms stored under the same output on `podman images` , and then when you use `podman run` if the image does not match the platform then it just tries the next one cycling through the list.
```bash
REPOSITORY                        TAG         IMAGE ID      CREATED       SIZE          PLATFORMS
quay.io/fedora/fedora-silverblue  latest      7b232f6a3738  40 hours ago  5.44 GB    linux/aarch64:linux/amd64
```

If all of the images fail then we output something similar to what is already output when you try to run an image on the wrong platform
```bash
$ podman run -it 7b232f6a3738 bash
WARNING: image platform (linux/arm64) does not match the expected platform (linux/amd64)
{"msg":"exec container process `/usr/sbin/bash`: Exec format error","level":"error","time":"2025-07-16T18:11:26.462497Z"}
```

I don't think this would break existing workflows and it doesn't require the platform data in the manifest to be correct. Curious if I'm missing something though.

As far as I'm understanding platform metadata of the manifests is used regardless of correctness right now anyway so jus support for manifests would get things working 

The images with broken metadata are broken already 

IMHO broken legacy data shouldn't stop fixing this

As for unique ness 

Images always either get used against the host platform or against a explicitly chosen one so i believe the normal ux already disambiguates

> Many images have incorrect platform data

s/Many/Some/ I guess and that would need some statistical data to see how common this still is. 


> Update local image storage to contain platform data but don't use it for anything yet. This way if/when we want to tackle this issue completely we will have the data easily available. And if the platform data isn't used for anything yet it won't break anything.

The image itself should already have this metadata but I think it pulls it from the config not the manifest and IIRC this is where there issues with incorrect data lies. The manifest may report something else then the image config.




> Ultimately I think it would be possible to have the different platforms stored under the same output on podman images , and then when you use podman run if the image does not match the platform then it just tries the next one cycling through the list.

> Images always either get used against the host platform or against a explicitly chosen one so i believe the normal ux already disambiguates

For run sure that can work, but what happens for `podman rmi`, `podman image inspect` and any command that takes an image as argument... If a name can resolve to two or more images then all this commands now need --platform/--arch options and then if something like `podman pull --arch arm64 X` `podman image inspect X` no longer works out of the box then it is clearly a major breaking change in behavior 

Adding an --arch option everywhere isn't certainly making it much easier to use the cli for multi arch use cases IMO.


There is a long complicated history here and I don't have time to dig the details out. This is clearly some larger complicated thing and that is also way beyond just what I think about this. If you really want to see some chnage I suggest this is best brought up at one of our community meetings https://podman.io/community

Well we already have the arch/platform  option in pull and run

Its just loosing tags all over the place 


Id recommend a manifest mode as opt in

If enabled tags refer to manifest lists and by default it only pulls the requested platform


For pull of alternative architecures it should prefer images of the preexisting manifest or prompt 

Edge cases exist in case of necessary image updates
/unassign
Pulling my name off this for now given the discussion, but feel free to ping me if there's something I can help with here
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

My main interest in this is to be able to pull a manifested image for multiple platforms and re-push to another repository. We have developers rebuild images and push to their own repos to test in MRs and then "bless" them once they're complete by pushing to the official repository. I'd really like to be able to support `podman pull --platforms all developer/repo:tag`, retagging, and `podman manifest push official/repo:tag`

@mathstuf That sounds like a job for skopeo instead, https://github.com/containers/skopeo.

Because podman doesn't store the on registry blobs. We decompress and then compress again potentially changed the digests, etc... skopeo can efficiently copy between two registries
its a bug that different platforms replace tags in the store
